segments.text: the Joe Rogan Experience.
>> After spending a year uncovering how my
house and my parents house burned down
and my neighbors burned alive and and
7,000 houses burned and then I realized
there's a a cover up going on, all the
negligence and I keep posting about it
and I have all the facts. I have all the
whistleblowers. of all the evidence and
business as usual and I see that nobody
is stepping up to run against the mayor
who's responsible for this disaster and
so many other disasters. So it became to
the point where I got so sick of just
being a as the younger people say in the
comment section a yapper. Like I felt
like I was just yapping. I'm like I'm
making these videos. I'm telling the the
truth. I'm
>> I got a congressional investigation. I
went to Washington. And I met with
everyone possible that I could do as
just a citizen.
And I was like, "Okay, well, game on
now. I'm going to go into your into your
headquarters and just take your job and
then remove all these toxic entities
that are destroying our way of life in
Los Angeles."
>> So, let's start from the fire. Um, so
the narrative was, God, there was a lot
of terrible, stupid, fake narratives,
and one of them was climate change. That
was the craziest one. The climate change
is causing the fire. Look, I lived in LA
for
29, 30 years, whatever it was. And I
guess it was, yeah, somewhere around
there, maybe even more. Whatever it was,
uh, when I lived in LA,
fire season happened every year. It,
this is not climate change. This is not
some new thing over the last couple of
decades. I was evacuated three different
times. Uh, I used to live in Bell Canyon
and my neighbors, three of the homes
right across the right across the street
from my house burnt to the ground in
2018. The there's always been fires in
Los Angeles, but the lack of preparation
for the Palisades fires was astonishing.
The fact that the reservoir was empty
was criminal mismanagement. I mean, it
was just insanity that everybody knew
that we had fires, like massive fires,
that it was a dry place, and when the
Santa Ana winds would blow, if something
caught fire, it was a real problem. We
had known that forever. And when you
see all these people that are passing
the buck and moving the blame and then
the fund when they had that big charity
thing for the fire and you found out
that hundreds of millions of dollars was
raised, you know, if you're you're
looking at it in like a an a rational
person, a rational person would say,
"Oh, this is great. all these people who
lost their homes will have some funds
from this and they'll be able to
rebuild. And then you find out that the
money was given to what was it like 108
different NOS
>> 200 plus
>> 200 plus where that money got
distributed to these organizations these
supposed nonprofit organizations and
most of that money goes to overhead and
almost nothing goes to the people who
lost their homes. So to re rewind reind
start with what we thought, right?
>> We were told climate change and with the
climate change because I've spent hours
and hours arguing with people that will
argue with that. I go, "Okay, great. The
climate changes, right? So we're aware
of this dry weather. It hasn't rained.
So what should we actually be doing?
Should we just say, "Oh, everybody
should burn alive and houses burn down."
Or should we clear the dead brush?
Should we pre-eploy? Should we make sure
that both reservoirs have water in it?
So the idea that climate change is the
the get out of jail, burn everything
down excuse, it doesn't even add up. So
we know that, so let's make a
difference. And I went and met with the
chief of the US Forest Service and
talked to him for a few hours. This guy,
Chief Garcia, he's one of the most
famous fire chiefs from the Hot Shots.
And I quizzed him and he told me this
was not a surprise. He said they all
have a map. I forget the name of this
map that it goes to all cities and
emergency personnel. They have photos.
You look at them. He showed them to me.
Everything is bright red leading up to
January 7th. Bright red. They knew this
was coming to the point where Chief
Garcia had all of his firefighters on
the tarmac kitted up in their
helicopters. He said his whole team was
standing by their computers because it
was so obvious this fire was coming
based off of if you want to say climate
change because it was it had not rained.
It was record dry. So this idea that
they use that it's it's just an excuse.
And then the big one that everyone falls
for to this day that is the best
propaganda ever is hurricane winds. We
were told you know Newsome's doing the
thing and he's saying that winds come in
the hurricane. and he lit his hair on
fire. There was no hurricane winds in
the Pacific Palisades. The max wind
speed was 40 mph. And for the first 6
hours when the helicopters, the initial
attack when you put out the the fire, it
was max I think 27 mph. So they got
everyone with it's unprecedented, it's
hurricane winds, it's climate change, no
responsibilities. So now we go to fire
aid. This is this was another thing that
just woke me up to, you know, we always
heard about the homeless NGO scam and
the homeless industrial complex, but
living as a a fire victim and watching
all these celebrities go on stage. They
actually took fire victims from Aladena
on stage whose houses burned down and
they raised this $und00 million. And as
a victim, I'm thinking, okay, you know,
we're going to get a few thousand
dollars. That's nice. or you know, you
break a hundred million up, there should
be a a grand. You know, even FEMA and
these places, when you get that $1,000
check, it's helpful. You're like, "Oh, I
just lost everything." Every little
thousand adds up. So when that happened
and nobody I know anywhere got money and
Sue Pascow from circling the news, a
local journalist whose house burned
down, she spent months investigating,
calling up every single NGO. Who did you
give money to? Which which victim?
Nobody got money. And even the the law
firm that they hired to do the, you
know, the cover up for uh the fire aid,
the law firm says in their own little
three-page document where they're
defending fire aid, they say several of
the money went directly to fire victims.
Well, I Google just to see because I
know the definition of several. I want
to see what does Google say. Several is
it was definitely under 10. So out of
200 plus
NOS's their own lawyers are saying
several gave to fire victims and then
you look at the three that they name
like we gave gift cards to victims which
victims which you were just handing out
if you were but it it was that that woke
me up to if they stole the money.
>> Yeah. And if they'll do that to the
people whose houses just burned down of
course they're going to do it to our tax
money with the homeless industrial
zombie complex. So that was a real wake
up that put me into, oh, here's where
the 2530 billion dollars goes. It
doesn't go to solving anything or fixing
it. It goes to scams. Well, I I don't
think before Doge and before Elon
started investigating into a lot of
these NOS's, I don't think anybody was
really aware or most people were not
aware of how this all works and how
there's a whole bureaucracy, like a
business that's set up where a bunch of
people get paid from this money to
essentially make no improvements
whatsoever in whatever the problem is,
whether it's homelessness. The
homelessness is one of the biggest ones
in LA because there was 24 plus billion
dollars spent on homelessness and when
people when representatives have tried
to do an audit to find out where this
money went, Nuome has blocked it. He has
vetoed this audit.
>> So it's it's even worse in the sense
that it's not going to just their
salaries. There's actual cases now with
the DOJ and the feds. They're arresting
people who are just stealing 30 million,
20 million, buying Bentleys, mansions in
Brentwood. So, the idea that it's just
going to salaries and people are paying
themselves out, that's one. But there's
also people just straight up stealing
money and and you can't even figure out
how they steal it. For instance, uh this
lovely lady um came on my podcast and
she's she created her own charity type
thing, the integrity project to expose
NOS's because she lives in Westwood and
all of a sudden one day on her block in
this, you know, she invested with her
husband, have a nice single family house
on this nice street in Westwood and the
old person home, they were kicking all
the senior citizens out and she's like,
"What's going on here?" And then next
thing you know, the building's on the on
the market for sale and it's for $11
million. 6 days later, that building
sells to a developer for $27 million.
Ends up this NGO, Weart, who's one of
the top I think they're at maybe a
hundred million just this year. They
haven't turned in their audit to the
feds. It's late right now. But for
instance, no one knows why it went from
11 million to 27 million over the
weekend in 3 days. So people pocket that
money. Here's the craziest part. Guess
who? So you the grant, you know, weard
gets a grant from the city or the state.
Guess who owns that building?
Not the city or the state. Weart. So our
tax money buys for 20 extra million
dollars of property to have it as a
homeless housing. Each of these beds,
cuz I think there's maybe only 70 beds
in it. It's now six years later
approximately totally not finished not
done more you know construction this or
that they still get paid as operators so
these NOS's not only get the money for
the grants to buy the building then they
get like a million dollars a year to be
operators and here's the best part
there's no mandatory that they have to
actually put a body in the beds so
>> you know so the scam is like I keep
saying this is a cartel this is mafia
this This is real mafia criminal stuff
going on. And the problem is, so one
thing I'm so excited to do when I'm
mayor and people in the comment section
like, "Oh, he's so stupid. You can't do
that." I've met with the IRS criminal
investigation team three times in LA,
twice in Washington DC, and they are so
excited for me to be mayor because all
they need is one document from each of
these NOS's and these grants and they
can open these investigations on fraud.
right now they know the fraud and the
and the crimes are happening but if the
city doesn't hand over the document and
the NGO doesn't they say they can't just
open up these cases without that one
document. So first week,
>> sorry there.
>> So for first week as mayor, I'm bringing
in the criminal investigation team of
the IRS. Here's all the NOS's we're
working with. I guarantee you 95% of
them already just call and they're like,
"Oh, Mayor Pratt, we're good. We're
actually going to Seattle. We don't want
to work here." Once they know someone's
coming to stop the cookie jar stealing
and then when people are like, "Oh, LA
has no money. How are you going to do
all this stuff?" LA has plenty of money
that we're just letting our tax money
just be stolen. And to increase a
problem, homeless since our current
mayor Karen Bass has has joined the city
power, she's increased homeless. They
reference numbers. They reference
numbers that she'll be like, "Oh, we
removed 1500 people this year." But she
doesn't say, "Oh, 1,500 were removed
into the cemetery because they owe
deed." Not to mention how much tax money
we're spending on just keeping zombies
alive. I met with firefighters a few
days ago at the Hollywood station and
they were telling me the amount of
Narcan they go through. So in one night
in the at the I talked to a m MacArthur
Park there fire station. He did 17
overdoses in one night.
>> Jeez. So if we they're not there given
the nar where the the amount of people
dying is even more insane. Right now six
people are dying a day in the street and
then they say oh this is compassionate
these people have rights. No these
people do not have rights for to just
die. We need to protect these people as
humans. And again that's why my whole
thing is enforce the law. It is illegal
to just be doing fentanyl on the street.
So, if we come in and we give you
mandatory treatment, not jail, if you're
not, you know, some of these people are
just straight going to jail for animal
abuse. They're torturing animals all day
long on Skid Row. The videos that I get
sent once you see them, you can't unsee
them. Not to mention, now I'm working
with all the rescue ones, the ones they
text me and they're just like, "Spencer,
we have to stop this." And the city
knows. They call the cops all day long.
The cops come and they say, I mean,
LAPD's hands are tied. If the mayor and
the city attorney, they don't have like
enforce the law, they just get away with
it. So, we're in Mad Max life in Los
Angeles. And people like to say, "Oh,
it's no, it's I'm from LA. I've grown
up." And I keep saying, "I'm fighting to
get LA back to what I grew up. It was
beautiful. It's why I wanted to be on a
TV show and be famous and be part of
Hollywood. It was magical." Not even
mention Hollywood is now gone. The fact
that Hollywood Boulevard should be the
greatest tourist attraction in the
world. You couldn't pay me right now to
go on Hollywood Boulevard, step on human
feces, the smell of pee, inhaling fent.
They're just everyone can just smoke
fentanyl in the streets now. It's
psycho. So again, why did I once you
start digging in and you spend all your
life now exposing this? Because again,
they burn my house down. They burn my
mom's house down. I have to. They put me
in the game. And once you the bubble's
gone, I just all I have is this energy
to stop this. Not to mention now the
amount of thousands of messages I get
every day from every part of the city
sending me photos. There's parents that
when they drive to school all across the
city, this is not just one unique area.
They have to have their kids in the back
seat staring at an iPad not to look out
the window because meth addicts will
just be having sex on the side of the
street. There's just naked people
everywhere. Now, and when I say people,
naked zombies. And the DEA will tell you
90% of these homeless people have a drug
problem. We have a drug addict problem.
These aren't people that just like
missed a paycheck and we need to get
them help and get back. This is a drug
problem that needs mandatory treatment.
not handing people needles and pipes and
saying, "Oh, here's a million-dollar
bed." If you're a fentanyl zombie
hanging upside down, you don't care
about a million-dollar empty bed cuz
you're just high. You sober up and you
go get high again. But what were we
talking about? Got me pumped up.
>> Well, it's good. It's good to be pumped
up.

sentences.original: I realized there's a cover up going on, all the negligence and I keep posting about it and I have all the facts.
sentences.translation: 我意識到正在發生掩蓋真相的行為，所有的疏失，而我持續發文揭露，並且掌握所有事實。
sentences.notes: 'Cover up' is a very common phrasal noun meaning a deliberate concealment of wrongdoing. 'Keep + verb-ing' expresses persistent ongoing action.

sentences.original: I see that nobody is stepping up to run against the mayor who's responsible for this disaster.
sentences.translation: 我看到沒有人站出來競選對抗這位對災難負責的市長。
sentences.notes: 'Step up' means to take initiative or rise to a challenge. 'Run against' means to compete against in an election. Both are extremely common in political and everyday contexts.

sentences.original: I got so sick of just being a yapper. Like I felt like I was just yapping.
sentences.translation: 我真的受夠了只是當一個嘴砲仔。我感覺自己只是在喋喋不休。
sentences.notes: 'Yapper/yapping' is contemporary slang for someone who talks a lot but takes no action. 'Get sick of' = become tired of/fed up with, an extremely useful everyday expression.

sentences.original: Okay, well, game on now. I'm going to go into your headquarters and just take your job.
sentences.translation: 好吧，那就開戰吧。我要直接殺進你的總部，把你的職位搶過來。
sentences.notes: 'Game on' is a colloquial expression signaling that a challenge or competition has officially begun. Very common in sports, business, and casual conversation.

sentences.original: This is not some new thing over the last couple of decades. I was evacuated three different times.
sentences.translation: 這不是過去幾十年才出現的新問題。我曾三次被疏散撤離。
sentences.notes: 'Not some new thing' is a dismissive sentence pattern used to argue something is not unprecedented. 'Over the last couple of decades' is a very natural time expression worth memorizing.

sentences.original: The lack of preparation for the Palisades fires was astonishing.
sentences.translation: 對帕利塞德火災的準備不足令人震驚。
sentences.notes: 'The lack of + noun' is a high-frequency formal structure for criticizing deficiencies. 'Astonishing' is stronger than 'surprising' and common in written and spoken formal English.

sentences.original: The fact that the reservoir was empty was criminal mismanagement.
sentences.translation: 水庫竟然是空的，這簡直是犯罪性的失職管理。
sentences.notes: 'The fact that + clause + was...' is a classic cleft-style sentence pattern for emphasizing a point. 'Criminal mismanagement' is strong professional/political language meaning recklessly negligent management.

sentences.original: You see all these people that are passing the buck and moving the blame.
sentences.translation: 你看到所有這些人都在推卸責任、轉移指責。
sentences.notes: 'Pass the buck' is a classic English idiom meaning to shift responsibility to someone else. Often paired with 'moving/shifting the blame.' Essential for discussing accountability.

sentences.original: A rational person would say, 'Oh, this is great. All these people who lost their homes will have some funds from this and they'll be able to rebuild.'
sentences.translation: 一個理性的人會說：『哦，這太好了。所有失去家園的人都能從中獲得一些資金，並且能夠重建。』
sentences.notes: 'A rational person would say...' is a rhetorical device used to contrast what *should* happen with reality. 'Be able to + verb' as an alternative to 'can' is important for expressing future ability.

sentences.original: The idea that climate change is the get out of jail, burn everything down excuse — it doesn't even add up.
sentences.translation: 氣候變遷是免責藉口、燒毀一切的藉口——這種說法根本說不通。
sentences.notes: 'Get out of jail (free) card/excuse' means a way to escape consequences. 'Doesn't add up' means something is illogical or inconsistent — both are essential idioms for skeptical argument.

sentences.original: So the idea that it's just an excuse. And then the big one that everyone falls for to this day.
sentences.translation: 所以這根本只是藉口。而且直到今天，還有一個大家都中計的說法。
sentences.notes: 'Fall for' means to be deceived by or believe something false. 'To this day' means even now, up to the present moment. Both are extremely common in spoken English.

sentences.original: That just woke me up to, you know, we always heard about the homeless NGO scam and the homeless industrial complex.
sentences.translation: 這讓我意識到，你知道，我們一直都聽說過遊民NGO詐騙和遊民工業複合體的問題。
sentences.notes: 'Wake up to (something)' means to become aware of or realize a truth, often an unpleasant one. Very different from physically waking up — a useful phrasal verb for discussions about awareness.

sentences.original: Every little thousand adds up. So when that happened and nobody I know anywhere got money.
sentences.translation: 每一筆小額的一千元都會累積起來。但當那件事發生後，我認識的人沒有一個拿到錢。
sentences.notes: 'Add up' here means to accumulate into a meaningful total. 'Every little [amount] adds up' is a very natural everyday expression encouraging appreciation of small gains.

sentences.original: There's also people just straight up stealing money and you can't even figure out how they steal it.
sentences.translation: 還有人就是明目張膽地偷錢，你甚至搞不清楚他們是怎麼偷的。
sentences.notes: 'Straight up' is a colloquial intensifier meaning directly, bluntly, or without question. 'Figure out' means to understand or solve — one of the most common phrasal verbs in American English.

sentences.original: Once they know someone's coming to stop the cookie jar stealing, then when people are like, 'Oh, LA has no money,' LA has plenty of money.
sentences.translation: 一旦他們知道有人要來阻止這種掏空公款的行為，當人們說『洛杉磯沒錢了』，其實洛杉磯有的是錢。
sentences.notes: 'Hand in the cookie jar' / 'cookie jar stealing' is an idiom for petty or opportunistic theft of public/shared funds. 'When people are like...' is a quintessential modern colloquial way to report speech.

sentences.original: I guarantee you 95% of them already just call and they're like, 'Oh, Mayor Pratt, we're good. We're actually going to Seattle.'
sentences.translation: 我保證，95%的人馬上就會打電話來說：『哦，普拉特市長，我們沒問題。我們其實要搬去西雅圖了。』
sentences.notes: 'I guarantee you' is a very natural spoken phrase for expressing strong certainty. 'They're like...' used for reporting dialogue is one of the most common features of modern American spoken English.

sentences.original: It doesn't go to solving anything or fixing it. It goes to scams.
sentences.translation: 這些錢沒有用在解決任何問題或修復任何事情上，而是流向了各種詐騙。
sentences.notes: 'Go to + verb-ing' means to be allocated/directed toward a purpose. 'It goes to solving / it goes to scams' is a high-frequency structure for discussing how money or resources are used.

clozeExercises.blanked: I realized there's a ___ going on, all the ___ and I keep posting about it and I have all the facts.

clozeExercises.original: The idea that climate change is the get out of jail, burn everything down excuse — it doesn't even add up.

clozeExercises.original: That just woke me up to, you know, we always heard about the homeless NGO scam and the homeless industrial complex.
clozeExercises.blanked: That just ___, you know, we always heard about the homeless NGO ___ and the homeless industrial complex.

blanks.hint: phrasal verb (caused someone to become aware of an unpleasant truth)
noun (a dishonest scheme to defraud people)

clozeExercises.original: Every little thousand adds up. So when that happened and nobody I know anywhere got money.
clozeExercises.blanked: Every little thousand ___. So when that happened and nobody I know anywhere ___ money.

clozeExercises.original: There's also people just straight up stealing money and you can't even figure out how they steal it.

clozeExercises.original: I guarantee you 95% of them already just call and they're like, 'Oh, Mayor Pratt, we're good. We're actually going to Seattle.'
clozeExercises.blanked: I ___ you 95% of them already just call and ___, 'Oh, Mayor Pratt, we're good. We're actually going to Seattle.'

fsiDrills.originalSentence: I realized there's a cover up going on, all the negligence and I keep posting about it and I have all the facts.
fsiDrills.prompt: Replace 'a cover up' with 'a conspiracy'
fsiDrills.expectedAnswer: I realized there's a conspiracy going on, all the negligence and I keep posting about it and I have all the facts.

fsiDrills.originalSentence: I realized there's a cover up going on, all the negligence and I keep posting about it and I have all the facts.

fsiDrills.expectedAnswer: I realized there's a cover up going on, all the negligence, but I don't keep posting about it even though I have all the facts.
alternatives: I realized there's a cover up going on, all the negligence, but I stopped posting about it even though I have all the facts.

fsiDrills.originalSentence: I realized there's a cover up going on, all the negligence and I keep posting about it and I have all the facts.
fsiDrills.prompt: Someone asks: 'Why do you keep posting about this issue online despite the pressure?' Use 'cover up', 'keep posting', and 'I have all the facts' in your response.

alternatives: Because I realized there's a cover up going on. I keep posting about it because I have all the facts.
fsiDrills.explanation: 應答練習：練習使用 'cover up'（掩蓋真相）和 'keep + V-ing'（持續做某事）來表達基於事實、持續揭露不公的立場。

fsiDrills.originalSentence: I see that nobody is stepping up to run against the mayor who's responsible for this disaster.

fsiDrills.originalSentence: I see that nobody is stepping up to run against the mayor who's responsible for this disaster.

fsiDrills.originalSentence: I see that nobody is stepping up to run against the mayor who's responsible for this disaster.

alternatives: Unfortunately, nobody is stepping up to run against the mayor who's responsible for this disaster.

fsiDrills.originalSentence: I got so sick of just being a yapper. Like I felt like I was just yapping.

fsiDrills.originalSentence: I got so sick of just being a yapper. Like I felt like I was just yapping.
fsiDrills.prompt: Your friend asks: 'Why did you decide to take real action instead of just talking about problems online?' Use 'get sick of' and 'yapper/yapping' in your answer.

fsiDrills.originalSentence: Okay, well, game on now. I'm going to go into your headquarters and just take your job.

fsiDrills.originalSentence: This is not some new thing over the last couple of decades. I was evacuated three different times.

fsiDrills.originalSentence: The lack of preparation for the Palisades fires was astonishing.

fsiDrills.originalSentence: The lack of preparation for the Palisades fires was astonishing.

fsiDrills.originalSentence: The fact that the reservoir was empty was criminal mismanagement.

fsiDrills.originalSentence: The fact that the reservoir was empty was criminal mismanagement.

fsiDrills.originalSentence: The fact that the reservoir was empty was criminal mismanagement.
fsiDrills.prompt: Someone asks: 'What was the most damning single example of government failure during the fires?' Use 'The fact that...' to structure your answer.

fsiDrills.explanation: 應答練習：練習使用 'The fact that + 子句' 的強調句型，在政治或商業批評語境中有力地點出問題核心，凸顯特定事實的嚴重性。

fsiDrills.originalSentence: You see all these people that are passing the buck and moving the blame.

fsiDrills.originalSentence: A rational person would say, 'Oh, this is great. All these people who lost their homes will have some funds from this and they'll be able to rebuild.'

fsiDrills.originalSentence: A rational person would say, 'Oh, this is great. All these people who lost their homes will have some funds from this and they'll be able to rebuild.'

fsiDrills.originalSentence: The idea that climate change is the get out of jail, burn everything down excuse — it doesn't even add up.

fsiDrills.originalSentence: So the idea that it's just an excuse. And then the big one that everyone falls for to this day.

fsiDrills.originalSentence: That just woke me up to, you know, we always heard about the homeless NGO scam and the homeless industrial complex.

fsiDrills.originalSentence: That just woke me up to, you know, we always heard about the homeless NGO scam and the homeless industrial complex.

Every little dollar adds up. So when that happened and nobody I know anywhere got money.
fsiDrills.explanation: 替換練習：替換 'every little ___ adds up' 句型中的金額名詞，練習這個表達小額積累的高頻日常說法在不同場景中的應用。

fsiDrills.originalSentence: Every little thousand adds up. So when that happened and nobody I know anywhere got money.
fsiDrills.prompt: Expand the second sentence to be more specific and complete: 'not a single person I know received any money from the relief fund'
fsiDrills.expectedAnswer: Every little thousand adds up. So when that happened, not a single person I knew anywhere received any money from the relief fund.
alternatives: Every little thousand adds up. But when the relief came through, nobody I knew anywhere got a single dollar.
fsiDrills.explanation: 轉換練習：將口語化的片段句子擴展為更完整清楚的表達，練習在保留核心語意的同時，增加細節讓表達更具說服力。

fsiDrills.originalSentence: Every little thousand adds up. So when that happened and nobody I know anywhere got money.
fsiDrills.prompt: You're explaining why you became suspicious about how disaster funds were distributed. First mention how small amounts accumulate into large totals, then describe how no one you knew received anything.
fsiDrills.expectedAnswer: Every little thousand adds up. So when that happened and nobody I know anywhere got money.
alternatives: Every little thousand adds up — so when those funds were distributed and nobody I knew anywhere got any money, I knew something was very wrong.
fsiDrills.explanation: 應答練習：練習先用 'every little ___ adds up'（小額累積）建立數量論點，再以個人觀察說明資金去向可疑，形成有力的口語論證。

fsiDrills.originalSentence: There's also people just straight up stealing money and you can't even figure out how they steal it.
fsiDrills.prompt: Replace 'straight up stealing money' with 'outright embezzling funds'
fsiDrills.expectedAnswer: There's also people just outright embezzling funds and you can't even figure out how they do it.
alternatives: There's also people just openly stealing money and you can't even figure out how they steal it.
There's also people just blatantly misusing funds and you can't even figure out how they get away with it.
fsiDrills.explanation: 替換練習：將俚語強調詞 'straight up'（明目張膽地）加竊盜行為替換為更正式或近義的表達，感受口語與書面語之間的語體轉換。

fsiDrills.originalSentence: There's also people just straight up stealing money and you can't even figure out how they steal it.
fsiDrills.prompt: Change to past tense: people were stealing and no one could figure it out.
fsiDrills.expectedAnswer: There were also people just straight up stealing money and you couldn't even figure out how they stole it.
alternatives: There were people who were straight up stealing money, and nobody could even figure out how they did it.
fsiDrills.explanation: 轉換練習：將現在式改為過去式，注意 'there's' 改為 'there were'，以及情態動詞 'can't' 改為 'couldn't' 的時態一致性。

fsiDrills.originalSentence: There's also people just straight up stealing money and you can't even figure out how they steal it.
fsiDrills.prompt: A friend asks: 'Is it at least obvious when public officials are stealing from relief funds?' Respond using 'straight up' and 'figure out'.
fsiDrills.expectedAnswer: There's also people just straight up stealing money and you can't even figure out how they steal it.
alternatives: There are people straight up stealing money, and the worst part is you can't even figure out how they do it.
fsiDrills.explanation: 應答練習：練習使用俚語強調詞 'straight up'（毫無疑問地、明目張膽地）和極常用片語動詞 'figure out'（搞清楚、弄明白）描述公款挪用的不透明性。

fsiDrills.originalSentence: Once they know someone's coming to stop the cookie jar stealing, then when people are like, 'Oh, LA has no money,' LA has plenty of money.
fsiDrills.prompt: Replace 'cookie jar stealing' with 'embezzlement'
fsiDrills.expectedAnswer: Once they know someone's coming to stop the embezzlement, then when people are like, 'Oh, LA has no money,' LA has plenty of money.
alternatives: Once they know someone's coming to stop the corruption, then when people are like, 'Oh, LA has no money,' LA has plenty of money.
Once they know someone's coming to stop the graft, then when people are like, 'Oh, LA has no money,' LA has plenty of money.
fsiDrills.explanation: 替換練習：將俚語隱喻 'cookie jar stealing'（從餅乾罐偷糖，比喻掏空公款）替換為更正式的詞彙，練習口語成語與正式書面語之間的轉換。

fsiDrills.originalSentence: Once they know someone's coming to stop the cookie jar stealing, then when people are like, 'Oh, LA has no money,' LA has plenty of money.
fsiDrills.prompt: Rewrite the reported speech part ('when people are like...') using formal indirect speech instead.
fsiDrills.expectedAnswer: Once they know someone's coming to stop the cookie jar stealing, people will claim that LA has no money, but the truth is LA has plenty of money.
alternatives: Once someone comes to stop the cookie jar stealing, people who say LA has no money will be proven wrong — LA has plenty.
fsiDrills.explanation: 轉換練習：將現代口語引語方式 'people are like...'（人們就說...）改寫為正式間接引語，練習語體轉換與更書面化的表達。

fsiDrills.originalSentence: Once they know someone's coming to stop the cookie jar stealing, then when people are like, 'Oh, LA has no money,' LA has plenty of money.
fsiDrills.prompt: Someone insists: 'LA literally has no budget left for fire prevention.' How do you counter this, using 'cookie jar stealing' and showing that LA actually has money?
fsiDrills.expectedAnswer: Once they know someone's coming to stop the cookie jar stealing, then when people are like, 'Oh, LA has no money,' LA has plenty of money.
alternatives: LA has plenty of money — it's the cookie jar stealing. Once someone comes to stop it, you'll see LA was never actually broke.
fsiDrills.explanation: 應答練習：練習使用 'cookie jar stealing'（掏空公款）這個生動的成語隱喻，以及現代口語引語方式 'people are like...' 來反駁「沒有資金」的說辭。

fsiDrills.originalSentence: I guarantee you 95% of them already just call and they're like, 'Oh, Mayor Pratt, we're good. We're actually going to Seattle.'
fsiDrills.prompt: Replace 'I guarantee you' with 'I bet you'
fsiDrills.expectedAnswer: I bet you 95% of them already just call and they're like, 'Oh, Mayor Pratt, we're good. We're actually going to Seattle.'
alternatives: Trust me, 95% of them already just call and they're like, 'Oh, Mayor Pratt, we're good. We're actually going to Seattle.'
I assure you 95% of them already just call and they're like, 'Oh, Mayor Pratt, we're good. We're actually going to Seattle.'
fsiDrills.explanation: 替換練習：將強調確定性的口語短語 'I guarantee you'（我保證）替換為其他表達強烈自信的說法，感受各種表達在語氣強弱與正式程度上的細微差別。

fsiDrills.originalSentence: I guarantee you 95% of them already just call and they're like, 'Oh, Mayor Pratt, we're good. We're actually going to Seattle.'
fsiDrills.prompt: Convert the direct speech ('they're like...') to formal indirect reported speech.
fsiDrills.expectedAnswer: I guarantee you 95% of them already just call and say that they're fine and that they're actually moving to Seattle.
alternatives: I guarantee you 95% of them already just call the mayor and tell him they're good and heading to Seattle.
fsiDrills.explanation: 轉換練習：將現代口語引語方式 'they're like...'（他們就說...）改為正式間接引語，練習直接引語與間接引語之間人稱及語態的轉換。

fsiDrills.originalSentence: I guarantee you 95% of them already just call and they're like, 'Oh, Mayor Pratt, we're good. We're actually going to Seattle.'
fsiDrills.prompt: Someone asks: 'Do you think most business owners will stay in LA and cooperate with the new mayor?' Respond with strong certainty about what they'll actually do, using 'I guarantee you' and 'they're like'.
fsiDrills.expectedAnswer: I guarantee you 95% of them already just call and they're like, 'Oh, Mayor Pratt, we're good. We're actually going to Seattle.'
alternatives: I guarantee you most of them will just call and say they're leaving — they're like, 'We're good, we're going to Seattle.'
fsiDrills.explanation: 應答練習：練習使用口語確定短語 'I guarantee you'（我保證）搭配 'they're like...' 的現代對話引述方式，表達對他人行為的強烈預測。

fsiDrills.originalSentence: It doesn't go to solving anything or fixing it. It goes to scams.
fsiDrills.prompt: Replace 'scams' with 'administrative overhead'
fsiDrills.expectedAnswer: It doesn't go to solving anything or fixing it. It goes to administrative overhead.
alternatives: It doesn't go to solving anything or fixing it. It goes to corruption.
It doesn't go to solving anything or fixing it. It goes to waste.
fsiDrills.explanation: 替換練習：保留 'go to + 名詞/動名詞'（用於、流向）的核心結構，替換資金流向的終點，練習用此句型描述資源被錯誤分配的不同情境。

fsiDrills.originalSentence: It doesn't go to solving anything or fixing it. It goes to scams.
fsiDrills.prompt: Combine the two sentences into one using 'instead of': 'Instead of going to solving anything...'
fsiDrills.expectedAnswer: Instead of going to solving anything or fixing it, it goes to scams.
alternatives: It goes to scams instead of solving anything or fixing the underlying problem.
fsiDrills.explanation: 轉換練習：將兩個對比句子合併為使用 'instead of'（而不是）的單一句子，練習在保留 'go to + V-ing' 句型的同時，用連接詞強化對比效果。

fsiDrills.originalSentence: It doesn't go to solving anything or fixing it. It goes to scams.
fsiDrills.prompt: Someone asks: 'Where does the disaster relief money actually end up? Shouldn't it help people rebuild?' Use 'go to + verb-ing' to contrast where it should go with where it actually goes.
fsiDrills.expectedAnswer: It doesn't go to solving anything or fixing it. It goes to scams.
alternatives: The money doesn't go to solving the problem or fixing anything — it just goes straight to scams.
fsiDrills.explanation: 應答練習：練習使用高頻結構 'go to + V-ing / 名詞'（用於做某事 / 流向某處）的肯定與否定形式，清楚說明資金的預期用途與實際流向之間的落差。

title: Fires, Fraud, and Homelessness: Why Spencer Pratt Is Running for Mayor of LA
